highlights: Chicago In 1427, the Florentine government conducted an extensive and detailed account of the financial state of its citizens in what would be the first comprehensive modern tax assessment in Europe. Known as the Catasto, this archive represents the property holdings and net worth of every household in the city’s jurisdiction. The massive amount of data continued within the Catasto’s registers has provided the basis for a wide range of scholarly investigations. title: The Florentine Catasto of 1427
highlights: In 1427, Florence undertook an exhaustive survey of its populace and their possessions. This remarkable effort set  ...    The meticulous and diligent work of the clerks resulted in a comprehensive snapshot of Florentine society. The census encompasses both the humble and the influential, the impoverished peasants, and the proud patricians, all documented  ...    of warfare, aging, or crop failures. Tuscany is unique in the European landscape of the fifteenth century. What truly sets the Catasto apart from other contemporary fiscal surveys is its vastness, diversity, and accuracy. It captures  ...    trade, commerce, and cultural innovation. By 1427, Florence had solidified its position in the European economy. highlights: catasto fiorentino del 1427 Archivio elettronico con programma di ricerca sotto MS-Windows (Click here for an english version of this page) Nel 1427 il Comune di Firenze stabilì di riformare il proprio sistema di raccolta tributi: ogni capofamiglia avrebbe dovuto presentare una dichiarazione in cui elencava proprietà immobiliari, debiti, crediti e componenti il nucleo familiare.
